title: Work, Energy & Power
subject: physics
summary: This topic connects force and motion through work and energy.
SPTG often tests work done by forces, kinetic and potential energy, conservation of mechanical energy, and power.
Harder problems include friction, efficiency, and combining energy with kinematics or force.
coreConcepts: Work is done when a force causes displacement in its direction.
Kinetic energy depends on speed: faster motion means much larger kinetic energy.
Potential energy depends on position, usually height in a gravitational field.
Mechanical energy is the sum of kinetic and potential energy.
If no non-conservative force acts, mechanical energy is conserved.
Friction converts mechanical energy into heat, so mechanical energy is not conserved in that case.
Power is the rate of doing work or transferring energy.
Efficiency compares useful output energy or power to total input.
derive: Work is force times displacement in the direction of motion, so W = Fs when force is parallel to displacement.
From Newton's second law and kinematics, the work done by net force becomes change in kinetic energy, giving the work-energy theorem.
Gravitational potential energy is defined relative to height, giving U = mgh.
Mechanical energy is E = K + U.
Power is work per unit time, so P = W/t.
Since v = s/t, power can also be written as P = Fv when force and velocity are in the same direction.
formulas: Work at angle: W = Fs cosθ
traps: Work is zero if force is perpendicular to displacement.
Potential energy depends on height difference, not the path taken.
Mechanical energy is conserved only when non-conservative forces like friction are absent or ignored.
Doubling speed does not double kinetic energy; it makes kinetic energy four times larger.
Power is not the same as total energy; it is energy per unit time.
A body can have zero potential energy at one reference level and nonzero at another; only differences matter.
Do not forget signs when work is done against motion or by friction.

sptgSet.question: A force of 10 N moves an object 5 m in the same direction. Find the work done.
Find the kinetic energy of a 2 kg object moving at 3 m/s.
Find the gravitational potential energy of a 5 kg mass at height 4 m. Use g = 10 m/s^2.
A machine does 120 J of work in 6 s. Find its power.
A 4 kg object moves at 5 m/s. Find its kinetic energy.
A 2 kg object falls from height 10 m. Find its potential energy at the top. Use g = 10 m/s^2.
If a body starts from rest and reaches 4 m/s, what is the change in kinetic energy for mass 3 kg?
A 1 kg object drops from height 20 m. Ignoring air resistance, find its speed just before hitting the ground. Use g = 10 m/s^2.
A machine has input energy 500 J and useful output 400 J. Find efficiency.
A force of 20 N acts on an object moving at 3 m/s in the same direction. Find power.

hardSet.question: A 2 kg object slides down from height 15 m without friction. Find its speed at the bottom. Use g = 10 m/s^2.
A 5 kg object moving at 6 m/s is brought to rest by friction. Find the work done by friction.
A force of 50 N acts at 60° to the direction of motion while the object moves 8 m. Find the work done by the force.
A lift raises a 200 kg load through 5 m in 10 s. Use g = 10 m/s^2. Find the power.
A 3 kg block is pushed across a rough floor with friction force 12 N for 5 m. Find the work done by friction.
A car engine delivers 40 kJ of useful work from 50 kJ of fuel energy. Find efficiency.
A 2 kg body is thrown vertically upward with speed 20 m/s. Find the maximum height reached. Use g = 10 m/s^2.
A 1 kg object has kinetic energy 72 J. Find its speed.
A force does 300 J of work in 15 s. Find average power.
A 4 kg object starts from rest and gains 200 J of kinetic energy. Find its final speed.

pat3Set.question: A 2 kg block slides down a 5 m high frictionless track. Find its speed at the bottom using energy conservation. Use g = 10 m/s^2.
A 3 kg object moves at 4 m/s and then at 10 m/s. Find the work done on it.
A 5 kg object is projected upward with 30 m/s from ground level. Find its total mechanical energy at launch. Use g = 10 m/s^2.
A machine of power 500 W works for 3 minutes. Find total work done.
A 2 kg object moves up an incline and rises by 3 m while friction does -20 J of work. If its initial kinetic energy is 100 J, find final kinetic energy. Use g = 10 m/s^2.
A 1 kg pendulum bob is raised by 0.8 m. Find the increase in gravitational potential energy. Use g = 10 m/s^2.
A motor lifts a 50 kg load at constant speed 2 m/s. Use g = 10 m/s^2. Find power output.
A 2 kg object falls from rest through 12 m. Find its kinetic energy just before impact, ignoring air resistance. Use g = 10 m/s^2.
A 3 kg block is pushed 4 m by a horizontal 25 N force on a rough surface. Friction is 10 N. Find the net work done.
A particle has total mechanical energy 200 J and potential energy 80 J at some point. Find kinetic energy at that point.
